questions.question: What happens in the brain during a seizure?
options: A sudden burst of intense electrical activity briefly disrupts how the brain normally works
The brain's blood supply is completely cut off
A permanent injury forms on the surface of the brain
The brain shrinks in size for a few minutes

questions.question: How is epilepsy best described?
options: A condition caused only by physical head injuries
A type of mental illness that people put on for attention
A condition that gives a person a tendency to have seizures
A short-term infection that clears up within days

questions.question: According to the course, how is a diagnosis of epilepsy usually made?
options: By a specialist doctor, usually after more than one seizure
By the person themselves once they feel unwell
By a care worker after watching a single seizure
Automatically after any one seizure of any kind

questions.question: Which of these does the course name as a group of seizure where the whole brain is involved from the start?
options: Focal aware seizures
Photosensitive auras
Generalised seizures
Focal impaired awareness seizures

questions.question: Which description best fits a tonic-clonic (convulsive) seizure?
options: The person loses consciousness, their body stiffens and then their muscles jerk rhythmically
The person has a headache with no change in movement or awareness
The person briefly stares and blanks out for a few seconds
The person notices a strange smell but stays fully aware

questions.question: In a tonic-clonic seizure, what happens during the tonic phase?
options: The person's body goes stiff, often causing them to fall if standing
The person recovers and feels fully alert
The person's muscles jerk rhythmically
The person quietly daydreams for a few seconds

questions.question: Which seizure type is described as a brief blanking out that is more common in children?
options: Focal aware seizure
Absence seizure
Status epilepticus
Tonic-clonic seizure

questions.question: Why can an absence seizure be easy to miss?
options: It only affects people who are asleep
It is brief and quiet, lasting only a few seconds with the person appearing to daydream
It always happens during the night
It causes loud convulsions that distract onlookers

questions.question: Where does a focal (partial) seizure start?
options: In just one part of the brain
In both sides of the brain at once from the start
In the heart before reaching the brain
In the spine and never in the brain

questions.question: What is the key difference between a focal aware and a focal impaired awareness seizure?
options: How long the person has had epilepsy
Whether the seizure happens indoors or outdoors
The person's age when first diagnosed
Whether the person stays aware during the seizure

questions.question: During a focal aware seizure, the person:
options: Stays conscious and can often describe it afterwards
Never notices anything unusual
Is unable to remember anything about it
Always loses consciousness completely

questions.question: Someone having a focal impaired awareness seizure is plucking at their clothing and mumbling. What is the right way to understand this?
options: They are drunk and should be left alone
They are being deliberately difficult
They are pretending to gain attention
They are not in control of it and will usually not remember it

questions.question: What is an aura?
options: A machine used to time seizures
A medicine given after a seizure
A type of brain scan
A warning that a seizure is coming, which is itself a focal aware seizure

questions.question: A person tells you they can feel a seizure coming. What is the best response?
options: Tell them to carry on as normal
Ignore it, as auras are never reliable
Leave them alone to deal with it themselves
Take it seriously, help them to a safe place and stay with them

questions.question: Which of these is named in the course as a common seizure trigger?
options: Drinking plenty of water
Going for a gentle daily walk
Missing doses of prescribed anti-seizure medication
Eating regular, balanced meals

questions.question: What does the course say about flashing or flickering lights as a trigger?
options: They trigger seizures in everyone who has epilepsy
They are the most common trigger of all
They can cure epilepsy in some people
They trigger seizures in only a minority of people with epilepsy

questions.question: A person falls and begins a convulsive seizure on the floor. What should you do first?
options: Hold their arms and legs still to stop the jerking
Lift them onto a chair straight away
Put a spoon or your fingers into their mouth
Cushion their head, move nearby objects that could harm them, and note the time

questions.question: When should you move a person who is having a convulsive seizure?
options: Always, straight into another room
Only if they are in a dangerous place, such as a road or by a fire
Never, under any circumstances
Only if other people ask you to

questions.question: Why should you never put anything in the mouth of someone having a seizure?
options: It can break teeth, injure them or you, and block their airway
It stops the seizure too quickly
It makes the person swallow their tongue
It is only unnecessary but otherwise harmless

questions.question: Once the jerking movements have stopped, what should you do?
options: Gently roll the person onto their side into the recovery position
Sit them upright in a chair immediately
Give them food and drink straight away
Leave them lying flat on their back and walk away

questions.question: Why is the recovery position used after the convulsive movements stop?
options: It stops the person from ever having another seizure
It helps keep the airway clear and lets saliva drain from the mouth
It makes the person wake up instantly
It is only for comfort and has no safety purpose

questions.question: How long must a convulsive seizure last before you should call 999, if there is no care plan saying otherwise?
options: More than 20 minutes
More than 30 seconds
More than 5 minutes
More than 1 hour

questions.question: What is the name for a convulsive seizure lasting longer than 5 minutes, or repeated seizures without recovery in between?
options: A focal aware seizure
An aura
Status epilepticus
An absence seizure

questions.question: Who may give rescue medication such as buccal midazolam?
options: Any care worker who has completed epilepsy awareness training
Anyone, as it is completely safe
Any family member who is present
Only staff who are trained, assessed as competent, and have the person's individual protocol

questions.question: What is the best summary of your role in supporting someone with epilepsy?
options: Restrain the person to keep them still until the seizure stops
Support the person, give safe seizure first aid, recognise emergencies and escalate, and record accurately
Give rescue medication whenever a seizure happens
Diagnose the type of epilepsy and adjust their medication

questions.question: One seizure on its own always means a person has epilepsy.
options: True
False

questions.question: Epilepsy is more common in people with a learning disability.
options: True
False

questions.question: During a focal impaired awareness seizure, a person may wander or fumble with their clothing and not be aware of what they are doing.
options: True
False

questions.question: A person can swallow their tongue during a seizure, so you should hold their mouth open.
options: True
False

questions.question: You should give a person food or drink as soon as the jerking stops, before they are fully alert.
options: True
False

questions.question: You should never leave a person alone during a seizure.
options: True
False

questions.question: Any care worker who has completed epilepsy awareness training is allowed to give buccal midazolam rescue medication.
options: True
False

questions.question: The risk of SUDEP is thought to be lower when seizures are better controlled.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are correct first aid actions during a convulsive seizure? (Select all that apply)
options: Hold the person's limbs still to stop the jerking
Put something between their teeth
Time how long the seizure lasts
Move harmful objects away from the person
Cushion the person's head with something soft

questions.question: Which of these should you NEVER do during a seizure? (Select all that apply)
options: Stay calm and reassure them
Put your fingers or an object in their mouth
Restrain the person or hold them down
Cushion their head

questions.question: Which of the following are named in the course as common seizure triggers? (Select all that apply)
options: Lack of sleep
Stress and illness
Drinking plenty of water
Missed medication
Gentle daily exercise

questions.question: When should you call 999 for a seizure? (Select all that apply)
options: One seizure follows another without the person recovering in between
It is the person's first-ever seizure
A convulsive seizure lasts more than 5 minutes
The seizure stops on its own within two minutes and the person recovers

questions.question: Which signs might you see during a tonic-clonic seizure? (Select all that apply)
options: Going pale or bluish around the lips
Staying fully alert and chatting throughout
Biting their tongue
Losing control of their bladder or bowels

questions.question: What should a person's epilepsy care plan typically include? (Select all that apply)
options: A guarantee that they will never have another seizure
How long their seizures normally last
What their seizures usually look like
Their known triggers and when to call for help

questions.question: Which of the following are part of giving dignified, person-centred support after a seizure? (Select all that apply)
options: Reassuring the person that they are safe
Bombarding them with questions while they are still confused
Helping discreetly if they have been incontinent
Keeping onlookers back to give privacy

questions.question: During a seizure you must never ___ the person or put anything in their mouth.
A convulsive seizure that lasts longer than ___ minutes is a medical emergency requiring a 999 call.
Once the jerking movements have stopped, gently roll the person onto their side into the ___ position.
A warning that a seizure is coming is called an ___, and it is itself a focal aware seizure.
SUDEP stands for Sudden Unexpected ___ in Epilepsy.
